binaries.title: vhdmp.sys - Kernel-pointer information disclosure in dependent-disk / STORAGE_DEPENDENCY_INFO query handler (sub_1C008F040)

changed_functions.name: sub_1C008F040
key_changes: Patched adds a new caller-provenance check at function entry: `call 0x1c0011800; test eax,eax; setne cl; mov [rsp+0x98],ecx` — a boolean (`rcx` in pseudo-C) meaning 'untrusted/user-mode requestor'.

Every store of a kernel object pointer into the caller's output-buffer entry is now guarded: `mov rax,<kptr>; cmp byte [rbp+0x40],r8b; cmovne rax,r8(=0); mov [rsi+0x28],rax`. When the provenance flag AND the arg2+0x40 flag are set, the kernel pointer is replaced with NULL.
Guard applied at all disclosure sites: primary entry field +0x28 (node pointer i_5) and +0x30 (i_5[0x88] object), and in the chained-entry loop at +0x20 (i_2/i_5 node) and +0x28 (entry[0x88] object).
Unpatched code unconditionally writes raw kernel pointers: `mov qword [r14+0x28], r13` (r13 = dependency-chain node) and `mov qword [r12+0x28], rax` (rax = node[0x88] object pointer).

Debug/telemetry message IDs and helper thunks were also re-numbered (0x1c005d6e0→0x1c005adc8, sub_1c00191c4→sub_1c0019204, sub_1c002aa60→sub_1c002aaa0, sub_1c001e088→sub_1c001e0c8), i.e. version drift, not the security change.
changed_functions.security_notes: Pre-patch, this dependent-disk/STORAGE_DEPENDENCY_INFO query handler copies raw kernel-mode heap pointers (the internal VHD dependency-chain list node and its associated object pointer at node+0x88) directly into the user-supplied output buffer. A low-privileged caller that issues the query IOCTL against a VHD device object receives these kernel addresses, defeating KASLR and providing pointers usable as targets/anchors for a subsequent memory-corruption exploit. The patch conditionally zeroes these pointer fields when the requestor is untrusted (sub_1c0011800() != 0) and the arg2+0x40 request flag is set.
changed_functions.assembly_diff:
; UNPATCHED sub_1c008f040 (verbatim, '-' lines from diff_function_diff forms=[assembly]) — key basic blocks:
mov     qword [rsp+0x10], rdx
push    rbx
push    rbp
push    rsi
push    r12
push    r13
push    r14
push    r15
sub     rsp, 0x40
mov     r15, rdx            ; r15 = arg2 (request/context object)
mov     rbx, rcx            ; rbx = arg1 (target/device object)
lea     rbp, [rel 0x1c0068048]
mov     r14d, 0x7
cmp     qword [rel 0x1c0068048], rbp
lea     r13, [rel 0x1c005d6e0]
je      0x1c008f0a1
mov     rax, qword [r15+0xb8]   ; rax = output buffer descriptor
mov     qword [rsp+0x90], rax
cmp     dword [rax+0x10], 0x4   ; version/level check
... 
mov     r12, qword [r15+0x18]   ; r12 -> input/output struct
mov     ecx, dword [r12]        ; class/version (1,2,3)
lea     eax, [rcx-0x1]
cmp     eax, 0x2
ja      0x1c008f5eb             ; invalid class -> STATUS_INVALID_LEVEL
... size computation ...
mov     eax, dword [rax+0x8]    ; rax = caller output buffer length
cmp     eax, ebp                ; compare with required size (rbp)
jb      0x1c008f583             ; too small -> BUFFER_OVERFLOW/TOO_SMALL
; ---- VULNERABLE DISCLOSURE SITES (unconditional kernel-pointer writes) ----
mov     qword [r14+0x28], r13   ; leak: entry+0x28 = kernel dependency-node ptr (r13)
cmp     dword [r12], 0x3
jne     0x1c008f4c6
mov     rax, qword [r13+0x440]
...
mov     qword [r14+0x30], rax   ; leak: entry+0x30 = node[0x88] object ptr
sub     ...
mov     qword [r12+0x20], r13   ; leak: chained entry+0x20 = kernel node ptr
mov     rax, qword [r13+0x440]
mov     qword [r12+0x28], rax   ; leak: chained entry+0x28 = object ptr
mov     qword [r14+0x20], rbx   ; leak: chained entry+0x20 = node ptr (rbx)
...
mov     qword [r15+0x38], rcx   ; *(arg2+0x38) = bytes written
mov     rcx, qword [rsp+0x30]
call    0x1c000e354
...
mov     eax, esi                ; return status
add     rsp, 0x40
pop     r15
pop     r14
pop     r13
pop     r12
pop     rsi
pop     rbp
pop     rbx
retn
; NOTE: unpatched has NO 'call 0x1c0011800' provenance check and NO cmovne-to-NULL guard;
; patched inserts: call 0x1c0011800; test eax,eax; setne cl; mov [rsp+0x98],ecx and
; at each site: cmp byte [rbp+0x40],r8b; cmovne rax,r8(=0); mov [dst], rax.

security_findings.entry_point: IRP_MJ_DEVICE_CONTROL on the vhdmp VHD device object — dependent-disk / STORAGE_DEPENDENCY_INFO query IOCTL (GetStorageDependencyInformation / IOCTL_STORAGE_QUERY_DEPENDENT_DISK path)
security_findings.vulnerability_class: Kernel-pointer information disclosure (CWE-200 / CWE-201) — uninitialized/raw pointer written to user output buffer, KASLR bypass
security_findings.description: sub_1C008F040 builds a dependent-disk information structure describing the VHD parent/dependency chain and copies it into the caller-supplied output buffer referenced by *(arg2+0xb8). For each entry in the dependency linked list (walked via node[0x95]/node+0x4a8) the pre-patch code writes RAW kernel-mode pointers into the output entry: the dependency-chain list node pointer itself is stored at entry+0x28 (`mov qword [r14+0x28], r13`) and its associated kernel object pointer node[0x88] at entry+0x30 (`mov qword [r14+0x30], rax`); the chained-entry loop stores the same class of pointers at entry+0x20 and entry+0x28. These are non-paged pool kernel heap addresses. The unpatched function performs no check on the caller's trust level before writing them, so any caller able to reach the IOCTL receives live kernel addresses. The patch adds `rcx = sub_1c0011800()` (an untrusted-requestor/previous-mode style check) at entry and, at every disclosure site, replaces the pointer with NULL when that flag and the request flag *(arg2+0x40) are set (`cmp byte [rbp+0x40],r8b; cmovne rax,r8; mov [dst],rax`).
security_findings.trigger_conditions: 1) Open a handle to a VHD/virtual-disk device object exposed by vhdmp (e.g. via OpenVirtualDisk on an attached VHD/VHDX, or directly opening the vhdmp device). 2) Issue the dependent-disk/STORAGE_DEPENDENCY_INFO query IOCTL with an input structure whose class/version field (*(arg2+0x18)[0]) is 1, 2, or 3 (values outside 1..3 return STATUS_INVALID_LEVEL). 3) Set the request so the *(arg2+0x40) flag (the 'include dependency object / detail' flag) selects the detailed record path, and provide an output buffer at least as large as the computed required size (>=0x10, and >= ((count*3)<<4)+8 for class 1/3 or (count<<6)+8 (+per-entry 2+name_len) for class 2) so the fill path (not the BUFFER_OVERFLOW/BUFFER_TOO_SMALL early-out) executes. 4) Read back the output buffer: entry offsets +0x28/+0x30 (and chained +0x20/+0x28) contain live kernel pointers. No race or special timing required; single IOCTL.
security_findings.primitive: Arbitrary kernel-address disclosure to a low-privileged user-mode caller: leaks the address of the internal VHD dependency-node object and a referenced kernel object (node+0x88). Defeats kernel ASLR and yields precise heap-pointer anchors that bootstrap a follow-on write/UAF exploit; not by itself code execution.
security_findings.assembly:
; UNPATCHED sub_1c008f040 @ 0x1c008f040 (verbatim key basic blocks, '-' lines from diff_function_diff forms=[assembly])
mov     qword [rsp+0x10], rdx
push    rbx
push    rbp
push    rsi
push    r12
push    r13
push    r14
push    r15
sub     rsp, 0x40
mov     r15, rdx            ; r15 = arg2 (request/context)
mov     rbx, rcx            ; rbx = arg1
lea     rbp, [rel 0x1c0068048]
mov     r14d, 0x7
cmp     qword [rel 0x1c0068048], rbp
lea     r13, [rel 0x1c005d6e0]
je      0x1c008f0a1
mov     rax, qword [r15+0xb8]   ; output buffer descriptor
mov     qword [rsp+0x90], rax
cmp     dword [rax+0x10], 0x4
jae     0x1c008f0e9
...
mov     r12, qword [r15+0x18]   ; input/output struct
mov     ecx, dword [r12]        ; class/version 1..3
lea     eax, [rcx-0x1]
cmp     eax, 0x2
ja      0x1c008f5eb
...
mov     eax, dword [rax+0x8]    ; caller output length
cmp     eax, ebp                ; vs required size
jb      0x1c008f583
; disclosure:
mov     qword [r14+0x28], r13   ; leak node ptr @ entry+0x28
cmp     dword [r12], 0x3
jne     0x1c008f4c6
mov     rax, qword [r13+0x440]
...
mov     qword [r14+0x30], rax   ; leak object ptr @ entry+0x30
mov     qword [r12+0x20], r13   ; leak node ptr @ chained+0x20
mov     rax, qword [r13+0x440]
mov     qword [r12+0x28], rax   ; leak object ptr @ chained+0x28
mov     qword [r14+0x20], rbx   ; leak node ptr @ chained+0x20
...
mov     qword [r15+0x38], rcx   ; *(arg2+0x38)=bytes written
call    0x1c000e354
mov     eax, esi
add     rsp, 0x40
pop     r15
pop     r14
pop     r13
pop     r12
pop     rsi
pop     rbp
pop     rbx
retn

breakpoints.why: Entry of the vulnerable dependent-disk query handler. rcx(arg1)=target object, rdx(arg2)=request/context. Inspect *(rdx+0x18) for the class/version dword and *(rdx+0x40) for the detail flag; *(rdx+0xb8) points to the output-buffer descriptor whose +0x8 = caller output length and +0x10 = level.

Unpatched block that executes `mov qword [r14+0x28], r13` — the raw kernel node pointer written into the output entry. r14 = current output entry base, r13 = dependency-chain node (kernel heap addr). Watch r13 and [r14+0x28]/[r14+0x30].

Provenance/trust check ADDED in the patch (absent path in unpatched). Break here in the PATCHED binary to see the boolean that gates disclosure; in the unpatched binary this call does not exist, confirming the missing check.

watch: r15 = arg2 (request/context object); *(r15+0x18) = pointer to class/version dword (must be 1,2,3)
*(r15+0x40) = detail/flag byte selecting the pointer-bearing record path
*(r15+0xb8)->+0x8 = caller output buffer length; must be >= required size to reach the fill path
r13 / rbx = kernel dependency-chain node pointers being written into output entries
output entry offsets +0x28 and +0x30 (primary), +0x20 and +0x28 (chained loop) = leaked kernel addresses
node[0x88] (i_5[0x88]) = referenced kernel object pointer also disclosed
key_offsets: 0x1c008f0e9: `mov ecx,[r12]` load of class/version; `cmp eax,0x2 / ja 0x1c008f5eb` is the 1..3 level validation
~0x1c008f4bd/0x1c008f532: `mov rax,[r13+0x440]` then store at entry+0x30/chained+0x28 — object-pointer disclosure
In PATCHED binary only: `call 0x1c0011800; test eax,eax; setne cl; mov [rsp+0x98],ecx` at prologue and `cmp byte [rbp+0x40],r8b; cmovne rax,r8; mov [dst],rax` at each store — the added guard
debugging_aids.trigger_setup: From user mode: attach/open a VHD (OpenVirtualDisk) or open the vhdmp device object, then call DeviceIoControl with the dependent-disk/STORAGE_DEPENDENCY_INFO query IOCTL (GetStorageDependencyInformation). Supply an input record whose class/version = 1, 2, or 3, set the detail flag corresponding to *(arg2+0x40), and pass an output buffer sized >= the computed requirement so the code fills entries rather than returning STATUS_BUFFER_OVERFLOW/STATUS_BUFFER_TOO_SMALL. Break on 0x1c008f040, step to 0x1c008f496, and read the output buffer after return.
debugging_aids.expected_observation: After the IOCTL returns STATUS_SUCCESS, the returned dependency-info entries contain 8-byte values at offsets +0x28/+0x30 (and +0x20/+0x28 for chained entries) that are canonical kernel addresses (0xffff8..../0xfffff.... range) pointing into non-paged pool — confirmed by `!pool`/`!address` resolving them to vhdmp-owned allocations. In the patched driver these same fields read back as 0 for the untrusted-caller path.
debugging_aids.notes: Distinguish safe vs vulnerable path by presence of the `call 0x1c0011800` at the prologue: unpatched lacks it. The class/version dword at *(arg2+0x18)[0] selects layout (1/3 = fixed-stride entries stride 0x30/0x40; 2 = variable with embedded name copied by sub_1c0012c40/sub_1c0012980). The disclosed pointer fields correspond to internal dependency-object references, not to the documented name/path string fields, so a benign client never needs them — reading them is the leak. Symbols are stripped; use the addresses above verbatim (image base 0x1c0000000).

summary: The patch to vhdmp.sys changes exactly one function, sub_1C008F040 — the dependent-disk / STORAGE_DEPENDENCY_INFO query handler reached from user mode via IRP_MJ_DEVICE_CONTROL. Pre-patch, the handler copies raw kernel-mode pointers (the internal VHD dependency-chain list node and its node+0x88 object pointer) straight into the caller-supplied output buffer at entry offsets +0x28/+0x30 (and +0x20/+0x28 for chained entries), giving any low-privileged caller a reliable kernel-address / KASLR-defeat information leak. The fix adds a caller-provenance check (sub_1c0011800()) and, combined with the request flag at arg2+0x40, replaces each disclosed pointer with NULL for untrusted callers. A PoC must open a VHD/vhdmp device handle and issue the dependency-info query IOCTL with class/version 1–3 and a sufficiently large output buffer, then read back the kernel pointers from the returned entries.
